Tab Bar Stance — R1

The charter's one open Liquid Glass question, isolated to a single variable. Same logging screen, same content, byte-identical — the tab bar's material is the only thing that changes. Content cards stay painted-light either way (locked); this page decides system chrome only: tab bar today, sheets by the same logic. Judge: does the glass frame earn its blur, or does the flat frame's quiet certainty read better on an instrument that's supposed to disappear into use.

A · Flat chrome

Resist — the instrument is one continuous painted surface.

9:41100%
Pillars · Week 3

↗Progress
➜Run
B · Glass chrome

Blend — native glass for chrome only; cards stay painted.

Both frames render the identical content markup — same header, same two cards, same set states — undercutting nothing but the tab bar. Frame A's bar reserves its own row: the active card stops flush above a hairline border, a hard architectural edge. Frame B's bar is undocked from flow and layered over content instead: the same card continues underneath it, its final set row — the now accent ring included — legible-but-blurred through the material, proof the translucency is doing real work, not sitting on an empty backdrop. (The done/current set order was deliberately adjusted from workout-logging-r1's Frame 1 so the now ring, not muted pending text, is the thing sitting at the content/chrome boundary — not a sign the two screens disagree.) Either way the charter's lock holds: content cards stay painted-light, never glass. Native SwiftUI gets the real .regularMaterial/.ultraThinMaterial; this HTML only approximates it with blur + alpha. Ben's call: flat or glass for tab bar and sheets.
